name: Palacios de Sanabria – tour around Eriá river and Truchas – Alto Peñón
name_original: Palacios de Sanabria – vuelta por río Eriá y Truchas – Alto Peñón
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Palacios De Sanabria are set within a diverse landscape in the province of Zamora, characterized by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and the presence of glacial lakes and reservoirs. The region's topography includes altitudes up to 1230 meters, providing varied gradients for cyclists. Notable features include the Sanabria Lake Natural Park, home to the largest glacial lake on the Iberian Peninsula, and the scenic Tera River Valley. The area offers a mix of paved roads and challenging ascents, making it suitable for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Palacios De Sanabria The most popular road cycling route is Palacios de Sanabria – tour around Eriá river and Truchas – Alto Peñón, a 66.1 miles (106.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 13 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Rio de Onor Village – Puebla de Sanabria loop from Puebla de Sanabria, a difficult 57.3 miles (92.2 km) path. This route offers views of local villages and the surrounding natural landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria – Hermitage of the Incarnation loop from Puebla de Sanabria, a 27.7 miles (44.6 km) trail leading through historic areas, often completed in about 2 hours 11 minutes. Road cycling around Palacios De Sanabria is defined by glacial lakes, river valleys, and mountainous terrain. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging ascents. The routes in Palacios De Sanabria are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 3 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Palacios De Sanabria's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Palacios de Sanabria?
A: There are over 30 dedicated road cycling routes around Palacios de Sanabria, offering a wide range of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these routes extensively, with more than 400 road cyclists using komoot to discover the area's varied terrain.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Palacios de Sanabria?
A: The region around Palacios de Sanabria is characterized by diverse and scenic landscapes. You can expect mountainous terrain with significant ascents and descents, picturesque river valleys like the Tera River, and routes offering views of glacial lakes, most notably within the Sanabria Lake Natural Park, and reservoirs such as the Cernadilla Reservoir.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Palacios de Sanabria offers numerous challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024435001/palacios-de-sanabria-tour-around-eria-river-and-truchas-alto-penon" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palacios de Sanabria – tour around Eriá river and Truchas – Alto Peñón&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 106.4 km route with over 1,500 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150340" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla de Sanabria – Lubián Village loop from Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, covering 115.6 km with over 2,200 meters of ascent.

Q: Can I find moderate road cycling routes in the area?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Palacios de Sanabria are classified as moderate, providing a balanced challenge. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40013154" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria – Hermitage of the Incarnation loop from Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, a 44.6 km route that takes about 2 hours and 11 minutes to complete, leading through historic areas.

Q: What are the best routes for scenic views?
A: Many routes offer spectacular scenery, particularly those that traverse the Sanabria Lake Natural Park or follow the Tera River Valley. The region's glacial landscapes, oak forests, and views over Lake Sanabria provide a beautiful backdrop for rides. Routes around the Cernadilla Reservoir also offer picturesque settings.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many of the routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148771" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rio de Onor Village – Puebla de Sanabria loop from Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 92.2 km path offering views of local villages and the natural landscape.

Q: What attractions or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes often pass by charming towns and historic sites. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/910086" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Counts of Benavente&lt;/a&gt;, or see the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3866268" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Incarnation&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also feature scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6142104" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;viewpoints&lt;/a&gt; overlooking the diverse landscapes.

Q: What is the best season for road biking in Palacios de Sanabria?
A: While specific weather data isn't provided, the region's mountainous terrain suggests that spring and autumn would offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the potential heat of summer or the colder conditions of winter at higher altitudes. These seasons also showcase the diverse ecosystems, from oak forests to chestnut trees, in their full beauty.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Palacios de Sanabria?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, which includes challenging ascents and scenic descents, as well as the stunning natural beauty of the glacial lakes, river valleys, and mountainous backdrops.

Q: Are there any routes that explore local villages or cultural sites?
A: Yes, several routes allow you to immerse yourself in the local culture. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla de Sanabria Castle – Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria loop from Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is a moderate 37.4 km ride that takes you past historic sites and through charming areas, including the historic center of Puebla de Sanabria.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families or beginners?
A: While many routes feature significant elevation, there are some easier options. Out of the 34 routes, 1 is classified as easy and 17 as moderate. These routes typically have less elevation gain and cover shorter distances, making them more accessible for families or those new to road cycling in mountainous areas.
tips: A beautiful little catholic church in Palacios de Sanabria. There is parking nearby and a few cafes if you want to get some food.

Muelas de los Caballeros, is a beautiful and well-preserved small town located at the foot of the Sierra de La Cabrera. The city's name derives from the local oak forests, some of which are several thousand years old.

Although the current name of this town is much clearer than that of its neighbor Castrocalbón, it is interesting to know that the origin of Castrocontrigo is in the primitive city of Aria, which was the one that gave its name to the Aria/Eria river, which crosses the municipality. , and in turn to the entire Valle del Aria, which ended up being known as Valdería.

At 14km long, Alto de Peñón is an incredibly beautiful climb where you will gain 715m with an average gradient of 4.9% and reach the top at an altitude of over 1800m. The views are spectacular as is the descent. Pedaling from east to west, the ascent begins in Truchas once the Eriá river has passed.
